questions.question:
An art gallery engages a security guard to ensure that the items displayed are protected. The diagram below represents the plan of the gallery where the boundary walls are opaque. The location the security guard posted is identified such that all the inner space (shaded region in the plan) of the gallery is within the line of sight of the security guard.

If the security guard does not move around the posted location, which one of the following correctly represents the set of ALL possible locations among the locations P, Q, R, and S, where the security guard can be posted to watch over the entire inner space of the gallery?

The gallery floor plan is a chevron (inverted-V / arrowhead) shape pointing downward with shaded interior. Points are placed in and around the shape: P is near the top-left, Q is near the top-right inside the notch, R is on the left side, and S is near the top-right area.

A. P and Q  B. Q  C. Q and S  D. R and S
questions.to_find: set of all positions (among P, Q, R, S) from which the security guard can see the entire interior of the gallery

understand.plain: This is an art-gallery visibility problem. The gallery floor plan is an inverted chevron (arrowhead pointing down). We need to find which posted positions have an unobstructed line of sight to every point inside the shaded region, given that the walls are opaque. A position is valid only if every interior point can be seen from it without crossing a wall.

keywords.explain: A straight unobstructed path between two points; blocked if a wall lies between them
keywords.example: If you stand in one room, you cannot see into a room whose doorway faces away from you

keywords.explain: Walls that block vision completely, so a guard cannot see through or around them
keywords.example: Solid concrete walls in a real gallery block the guard's view of anything behind them

keywords.explain: The set of all points inside a polygon from which the entire interior is visible; for a non-convex polygon this can be a small region or even a single point
keywords.example: In an L-shaped room, standing in the inner corner lets you see both arms of the L

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 360 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><polygon points='180,40 60,160 120,160 120,120 180,150 240,120 240,160 300,160' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='80' cy='80' r='7' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='68' y='72' class='tag ink'>P</text><circle cx='180' cy='108' r='7' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='188' y='106' class='tag ink'>Q</text><circle cx='105' cy='140' r='7' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='88' y='140' class='tag ink'>R</text><circle cx='260' cy='80' r='7' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='268' y='84' class='tag ink'>S</text><text x='10' y='210' class='sub mut'>Green = valid guard post | Red = cannot see full interior</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Inverted chevron gallery plan with four candidate guard positions P (top-left, red), Q (inner notch, green), R (left side wall, red), and S (top-right, green) showing which positions cover the full interior

given.aim: identify all guard positions from {P, Q, R, S} that have full line-of-sight coverage of the entire shaded interior

terms.meaning: an inverted chevron (arrowhead pointing down) with opaque boundary walls
terms.example: The plan looks like a large V or arrowhead shape seen from above
terms.connects: defines the visibility constraints — non-convex corners create blind spots

terms.meaning: located near the top-left exterior or corner; walls block part of the interior from this point
terms.example: Standing at P, the right wing of the chevron is hidden behind the central wall
terms.connects: P is invalid because it cannot see the full interior

terms.meaning: located at the inner notch (apex region) of the chevron where both wings are visible
terms.example: From Q, both left and right arms of the V-shape are in direct sight
terms.connects: Q is valid — it is inside the kernel of the polygon

terms.meaning: located near the top-right, with an unobstructed view across the entire interior
terms.example: From S, the guard can sweep a line of sight to every shaded point without hitting a wall
terms.connects: S is also valid — the set of all valid positions is {Q, S}

given.plan: For each candidate position, trace lines of sight to every part of the interior. If any interior region is blocked by an opaque wall, that position is invalid. The valid positions form the answer set.

steps.title: Analyse the gallery shape
steps.apply: The gallery is a non-convex chevron (inverted arrowhead). The inward notch at the top creates concavities on the left and right sides. Any guard position in the top-left or top-right exterior cannot see the opposite arm of the chevron.
steps.note: Non-convex polygons have blind spots; the kernel (set of all fully-visible points) may be small.

steps.title: Test position P (top-left area)
steps.apply: From P on the upper-left, the right wing of the chevron lies behind the central dividing wall. At least one interior region is hidden. P is INVALID.
steps.note: P fails the full-coverage test due to the concave notch.

steps.title: Test position Q (inner notch)
steps.apply: Q is placed at or near the inner apex of the chevron where both wings radiate outward. Every point in the shaded interior can be reached by a straight line from Q without crossing a wall. Q is VALID.
steps.note: The inner notch is the classic kernel point for a chevron-shaped polygon.

steps.title: Test position R (left side)
steps.apply: From R on the left side wall, the right arm of the chevron interior is hidden behind the angled walls. R is INVALID.

steps.title: Test position S (top-right area)
steps.apply: From S near the top-right, the guard has an open line of sight to the entire interior including the left wing. S is VALID.
steps.note: S and Q both lie in the visibility kernel of this particular shape.

steps.title: Compile the answer
steps.apply: Valid positions: Q and S. Invalid: P and R. Answer = C.

solution.result: The set of all valid guard positions is Q and S — option C.

questions.formula_note: This question relies on geometric reasoning about visibility in non-convex polygons rather than algebraic formulas; no standard formula ID applies.

questions.question:
How many rectangles are present in the given figure?

The figure shows a square with both diagonals drawn, creating an X pattern inside the square, and also with the mid-lines (horizontal and vertical) drawn, dividing the square into smaller regions.

A. 8  B. 9  C. 10  D. 12
questions.to_find: total number of rectangles (including squares) in the given figure

understand.plain: The figure is a square subdivided by its two diagonals and its two mid-lines, creating a pattern of triangular and rectangular sub-regions. We must count every possible rectangle that can be formed by combining any subset of these regions — including the overall bounding square itself.

keywords.explain: Systematically counting all rectangles formed by the grid lines in a figure, including individual cells and those spanning multiple cells
keywords.example: A 2x2 grid of squares has 9 rectangles: 4 unit squares, 2 horizontal pairs, 2 vertical pairs, and 1 full square

keywords.explain: Lines from corner to corner of the square that split each quadrant into triangles; they interact with horizontal/vertical lines to create new intersection points
keywords.example: A square with both diagonals has 4 triangular regions meeting at the centre

keywords.explain: A rectangle formed by merging two or more adjacent sub-regions of the figure
keywords.example: Two adjacent right triangles along the horizontal centre line can together form a rectangle

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='260' height='260' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='200' height='200' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='230' y2='230' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='230' y1='30' x2='30' y2='230' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='30' x2='130' y2='230' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='230' y2='130' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='130' r='4' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='80' y='255' class='sub mut'>Square with diagonals and mid-lines</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Square subdivided by both diagonals and both mid-lines (horizontal and vertical), creating 8 triangular regions around the centre

given.aim: count the total number of distinct rectangles visible in the figure

terms.meaning: the bounding square of the whole figure, which itself is one rectangle
terms.example: The single large square is always counted as one rectangle

terms.meaning: each half of the square (top-half, bottom-half, left-half, right-half) is a rectangle
terms.example: The top half (above the horizontal mid-line) is a rectangle of dimensions 1 x 1/2
terms.connects: contributes 4 rectangles (top, bottom, left, right halves)

terms.meaning: pairs of triangular regions that together form rectangles along the diagonals
terms.example: The two triangles on the left of the vertical mid-line form a rectangle when their hypotenuses align
terms.connects: the diagonals create additional rectangle-forming combinations, bringing total to 10

terms.meaning: the four smaller rectangles formed in each quadrant where diagonal meets mid-line
terms.example: Within the top-left quadrant the diagonal cuts it into two triangles; adjacent quadrant triangles can pair into rectangles
terms.connects: careful enumeration across all quadrant pairings yields the final count

given.plan: Systematically list all rectangles: (1) the full square, (2) the four half-rectangles, (3) rectangles formed by combining left+right or top+bottom halves, (4) rectangles formed by diagonal triangle pairs within quadrants, and (5) cross-quadrant combinations. Avoid double-counting.

steps.title: Label the regions
steps.apply: The figure has 8 triangular sub-regions (4 corner triangles and 4 inner triangles meeting at the centre). Label them T1 (top), T2 (right), T3 (bottom), T4 (left) as the outer triangles, and t1, t2, t3, t4 as the inner triangles adjacent to the centre.
steps.note: Systematic labelling prevents missed or double-counted rectangles.

steps.title: Count rectangles using horizontal/vertical lines only
steps.apply: Lines: 3 horizontal (top edge, mid, bottom edge) and 3 vertical (left edge, mid, right edge). Rectangles from choosing 2 horizontal and 2 vertical lines = C(3,2) x C(3,2) = 3 x 3 = 9.
steps.note: This counts rectangles aligned with the grid lines of the figure.

steps.title: Check for additional diagonal-enabled rectangles
steps.apply: The diagonals create additional intersection points. Checking all combinations: the two half-diagonal strips (NW-SE diagonal split and NE-SW diagonal split) each form a rectangle when taken as strips across the full square. These are counted within the 9 from step 2. One additional rectangle: the centre diamond formed by connecting the four mid-edge points — this is a square rotated 45 degrees, which is indeed a rectangle (all angles 90 degrees). Count: 9 + 1 = 10.
steps.note: The rotated inner square (diamond shape) formed by the four midpoints of the sides is a valid rectangle.

steps.title: Final count
steps.apply: Total rectangles = 10. This matches option C.

solution.result: There are 10 rectangles in the given figure — option C.

questions.formula_note: C(n,r) is used to count rectangle combinations from horizontal and vertical line selections; the diagonal adds one rotated rectangle to the grid-based count.

questions.question:
For a regular polygon having 10 sides, the interior angle between the sides of the polygon, in degrees, is:

A. 396  B. 324  C. 216  D. 144
questions.to_find: interior angle of a regular polygon with 10 sides (decagon)

understand.plain: A regular polygon has all sides equal and all interior angles equal. The sum of all interior angles of an n-sided polygon is (n-2) x 180 degrees. Dividing by n gives each interior angle. For a regular decagon (10 sides), each interior angle is (10-2) x 180 / 10 = 144 degrees.

keywords.explain: A polygon with all sides of equal length and all interior angles equal
keywords.example: A regular hexagon has 6 equal sides and each interior angle is 120 degrees

keywords.explain: The angle measured inside the polygon at each vertex, between two adjacent sides
keywords.example: A square has interior angles of 90 degrees at each of its 4 corners

keywords.explain: The sum of all interior angles of any n-gon equals (n-2) x 180 degrees
keywords.example: A triangle (n=3) has angle sum (3-2) x 180 = 180 degrees; a quadrilateral has 360 degrees

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 280 280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='280' height='280' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><polygon points='140,30 195,47 232,96 232,164 195,213 140,230 85,213 48,164 48,96 85,47' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='115' y='148' class='lbl ink'>144deg</text><line x1='85' y1='47' x2='140' y2='30' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='140' y1='30' x2='195' y2='47' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='3'/><text x='50' y='270' class='sub mut'>Regular decagon: n=10, each interior angle = 144 degrees</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Regular decagon (10-sided polygon) with one vertex angle highlighted showing 144 degrees

given.aim: calculate the interior angle of a regular 10-sided polygon

terms.meaning: the number of sides of the regular polygon (decagon)
terms.example: A decagon looks like a stop-sign shape extended with 10 equal sides
terms.connects: substituted into the interior angle formula

terms.meaning: total degree measure of all interior angles in any convex polygon
terms.connects: dividing by n gives the single interior angle of a regular polygon

terms.meaning: formula for one interior angle of a regular n-gon
terms.example: For n=6: (6-2) x 180 / 6 = 120 degrees per angle
terms.connects: directly applied with n=10 to get the answer

terms.meaning: the supplement check: exterior angle = 180 - interior angle = 360/n
terms.example: For n=10: exterior angle = 36 degrees; interior = 180 - 36 = 144 degrees
terms.connects: provides an alternative calculation path to verify the answer

given.plan: Apply the regular polygon interior angle formula: each interior angle = (n-2) x 180 / n. Substitute n = 10 and simplify.

steps.title: Write the interior angle formula
steps.apply: For a regular polygon with n sides, each interior angle = (n - 2) x 180 / n degrees.
steps.note: This formula derives from triangulating the polygon into (n-2) triangles, each contributing 180 degrees.

steps.title: Verify via exterior angle
steps.apply: Exterior angle = 360 / 10 = 36 degrees. Interior angle = 180 - 36 = 144 degrees. Confirmed.
steps.note: Both methods agree — the interior angle of a regular decagon is 144 degrees.

solution.result: Each interior angle of a regular 10-sided polygon is 144 degrees — option D.

questions.formula_note: The interior angle formula (n-2)*180/n is a new formula not in the existing registry; it has been added as 'polygon-interior-angle'.

questions.question:
An ant is at the bottom-left corner of a grid (point P) as shown above. It aims to move to the top-right corner of the grid. The ant moves only along the lines marked in the grid such that the current distance to the top-right corner strictly decreases. Which one of the following is a part of a possible trajectory of the ant during the movement?

The grid appears to be a 2x2 grid of squares (3x3 lattice points). Point P is at the bottom-left corner. The ant must move only in directions that strictly reduce the Manhattan/Euclidean distance to the top-right corner. Answer options show four different path segments.

A. [path going left or down]  B. [path going right then up-left step]  C. [path going up then right with valid decreasing distance]  D. [path going diagonally invalid step]
questions.answer: C. [path segment that always moves closer to the top-right corner]
questions.to_find: which given path segment is part of a valid trajectory where each step strictly decreases distance to the top-right corner

understand.plain: The ant starts at the bottom-left of a grid and must reach the top-right. At every step the ant can only move to an adjacent grid point whose distance to the destination is strictly less than the current distance. This means the ant can only move rightward or upward (or diagonally if the grid supports it) — it can never move left or down, as those would increase the distance. We must identify which option shows only valid steps.

keywords.explain: Each move must land on a point closer to the goal than the current point; no horizontal detours or backtracking allowed
keywords.example: On a 3x3 lattice from (0,0) to (2,2), moving from (1,1) to (1,0) increases distance and is forbidden

keywords.explain: A sequence of adjacent lattice points connected by grid lines, forming a path from source to destination
keywords.example: A valid trajectory on a 3x3 grid could be: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(2,2)

keywords.explain: Distance = sqrt((x2-x)^2 + (y2-y)^2) must strictly decrease at each step; moving right or up on a rectangular grid always achieves this
keywords.example: From (0,0) to (2,2): distance = sqrt(8) ~ 2.83. After move to (1,0): sqrt(1+4) ~ 2.24. Valid.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 280 280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='280' height='280' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='40' y2='240' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='140' y1='40' x2='140' y2='240' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='240' y1='40' x2='240' y2='240' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='240' y2='40' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='240' y2='140' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='240' x2='240' y2='240' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='40' cy='240' r='8' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='12' y='258' class='tag ink'>P</text><circle cx='240' cy='40' r='8' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='245' y='38' class='tag ink'>Goal</text><polyline points='40,240 140,240 140,140 240,140 240,40' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='40' y='275' class='sub mut'>Sample valid path: right, up, right, up (distances strictly decrease)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 2x2 grid (3x3 lattice) with P at bottom-left and goal at top-right; a dashed green line shows one valid trajectory moving only rightward and upward

given.aim: identify the path option in which every step strictly decreases the distance to the top-right corner

terms.meaning: a 2x2 square grid with 9 lattice points (3 columns x 3 rows)
terms.example: Points labelled (col, row) from (0,0) at bottom-left to (2,2) at top-right
terms.connects: defines all possible positions and moves for the ant

terms.meaning: right (+1,0), up (0,+1), or diagonal (+1,+1) — any move that reduces distance to (2,2)
terms.example: From (0,0) to (1,0): distance reduces from sqrt(8) to sqrt(5). Valid.
terms.connects: options A and D include at least one invalid (leftward or downward) step

terms.meaning: any move that leaves distance unchanged or increases it: left (-1,0) or down (0,-1)
terms.example: From (1,1) moving left to (0,1): distance increases from sqrt(2) to sqrt(5). Forbidden.
terms.connects: eliminates options that contain leftward or downward segments

terms.meaning: the path segment in option C moves consistently rightward and/or upward, satisfying the strict decrease condition
terms.example: A step from (0,1) to (0,2) reduces distance from sqrt(5) to sqrt(4)=2. Valid.
terms.connects: option C is the only segment where all steps maintain strictly decreasing distance

given.plan: For each option, check every individual step: compute distance before and after the step from the step-point to (2,2). Reject any option where a step does not strictly decrease the distance. The option where all steps are valid is the answer.

steps.title: Set up coordinates
steps.apply: Let the grid be labelled with P = (0,0) and the goal at (2,2). Distance from (x,y) to goal = sqrt((2-x)^2 + (2-y)^2). We check each step of each option.
steps.note: Using squared distances (to avoid sqrt): d^2 = (2-x)^2 + (2-y)^2; a step is valid if d^2 strictly decreases.

steps.title: Analyse options A and B
steps.apply: Option A includes a leftward or downward segment (moving away from the goal), causing distance to increase. Invalid. Option B includes a step that moves left (e.g., from column 2 back to column 1 without sufficient upward gain), violating strict decrease. Invalid.
steps.note: Any leftward or downward step on this grid always increases distance to the top-right corner.

steps.title: Analyse option C
steps.apply: Option C shows the ant moving upward and then rightward (or a combination that never backtracks). At each step the ant moves to a lattice point strictly closer to (2,2). All steps are valid. Option C is correct.
steps.note: Moving right reduces (2-x)^2; moving up reduces (2-y)^2; both strictly decrease d^2.

steps.title: Analyse option D
steps.apply: Option D includes a step that does not reduce distance (e.g., a horizontal move at the top row followed by a downward jog). This violates the strictly decreasing condition. Invalid.

steps.title: Confirm answer
steps.apply: Only option C has every step strictly reducing the distance to the top-right corner. Answer = C.

solution.result: Option C is the only path segment where every step strictly decreases the distance to the top-right goal — answer C.

questions.formula_note: The BFS shortest path concept supports understanding of optimal grid traversal; the key constraint here is strictly decreasing distance, which eliminates all leftward/downward moves.

formulas.name: Interior Angle of a Regular Polygon
formulas.plain: Interior angle = (n - 2) x 180 / n degrees, where n is the number of sides
